title: Why Sugar & Fruit Juice Are NOT The Same

chunks.title: Sugar Comparison and Gut Health
chunks.summary: This segment discusses the differences between pure sugar and natural sweeteners like honey and apple juice, particularly their effects on gut flora and endotoxin levels. The speaker explains how honey contains beneficial compounds that help mitigate negative health outcomes associated with processed sugars.
chunks.transcript: Based on what you're saying now, and this is I I hope it doesn't put you on the spot too much. I'm just genuinely curious like if you had to choose between like a pure like cane sugar soda and diet soda, what would it be now? >> This is one of these like unwininnable things, right? >> I know, right? Just curious like where are you now? >> You know, I don't I mean I I don't know what you would do in that situation. I don't drink either, you know? But what I will tell you is this. If you look at something like honey, the choice is very clear, right? Because I think that the goal is to create products for humans or to create like an an industry ethos where people don't have to make that choice. >> Yeah. >> What if you had a soda or a drink that was sweetened with honey? Well, then you're looking at a completely different situation because there are natural sugars. And I think that the mechanism here is that there are so many plant compounds in honey or even maple syrup that affect the gut flora. And I think the mechanism has to do with dispiosis and ultimately with lipopolysaccharide. And I've I've shown this in uh I've I've done some tweets about this on X. Um it's kind of complicated, but if you look at sugar, pure sugar versus apple juice, pure sugar increases endotoxin in the human body, probably because you put a bunch of glucose or fructose into the human gut and there there's nothing there to kind of check the overgrowth of bacteria. gram negative bacteria propagate and you're going to get more endotoxin like a polysaccharide which is a gram negative bacterial cell wall component into the blood circulation but you give someone apple juice and that doesn't happen and so this has actually been shown across multiple like sugar versus fruit and honey does the same thing you can give I think it's an animal study but they gave animals um two types of sugar and well excuse me two types of honey and processed sugar and the honey actually mitigated the rise in lipop polysaccharide that

chunks.title: Processed Foods and Convenience
chunks.summary: The conversation shifts to the convenience factor driving people's food choices, highlighting how processed food manufacturers exploit this need for quick and easy options. The speaker critiques the rising trend of 'protein' convenience foods and stresses the importance of creating healthier alternatives that are also convenient.
chunks.transcript: convenience because look it's one thing for me to sit in a studio with you and Okay, the answer is simple. Just go to the grocery store, eat a chicken breast, eat a steak, um, eat some salad if you want. I don't eat salad, but if people want to eat salad, great. Eat a sweet potato. But I think people say, "I don't have time for that." You know, I work. I have kids. And I get it. Like, we have constraints on our time. And so, I think that the way that these processed food manufacturers, and I will say David Bar is a ultrarocessed food manufacturer now. I don't think that they're doing the right thing. And I think that many and they're not the only ones. Like this idea of protein pop-tarts and protein cookies, this has exploded. Like these companies are very popular now, but it's convenient. And so that's where we get people. We say, "Oh, well, we're going to give you a a slightly less expensive option. They're still expensive, but we're going to give you a reasonably priced option that is convenient for you, and that's that's going to be helpful for you." When in fact, it's just kind of a fouian bargain. Like, no, we have to really fight against the convenience. But I know that the thing is that humans are just programmed to be convenient. And so we always go for this. And so like like I said, I think that the my hope is that more companies will just put products in the market that are better for humans. I mean that was that was really the intention and I never thought I would be in this position, but that was that was why I created Lineage. Like I wanted this this might sound weird, but I wanted to like push back against the process boot company somehow >> and give people like, okay, here's a convenient option that I actually believe in. Like when you look at the calories per gram on like airdried steak from Lineage like or just a lean jerky or even a chicken breast, they're just as good as a David bar and it is a micronutrient-rich food that I am, you know, my sense is like it's certainly going to leave you less hungry, more satiated long term. So like I think that we need more options for people. So, I hope someone will create, like you said, a drink that has honey, right, as a sweetener that doesn't mess up your gut flora and you won't have to make the decision between Diet Coke and sucrossse Coke, right? And that's what that's what I'm trying to do with, you know, like the ability that I have now to create. I want to create companies and products that give people a convenient option that's better because I think without that, we're we're we're screwed, right?

chunks.title: Honey as a Staple in Diets
chunks.summary: In this segment, the speakers discuss the role of honey in their diets, emphasizing its nutritional benefits over other carbohydrate sources. They consider honey a staple, advocating for its inclusion due to its favorable properties and potential health benefits when consumed appropriately.
chunks.transcript: source of carbohydrates. I mean, we were just drinking honey post-workout together, you know, and you you were telling me that you like honey in your diet now. So, honey's great. >> I I would consider it a staple legit, right? >> It's a staple. >> It's a staple >> because like I'm I enjoy I'm very deliberate about my carbohydrates and like realistically like there's not a whole lot on that list that I would eat, right? Because I'm I'm not a big grains guy. Sweet potatoes are great, but they do start to get to me after a while, right? >> How so? I just if I start eating them like consistently like after like two three weeks of doing you know 100 grams a day or you know worth of carbs a day of sweet potatoes I start feeling distended. I start feeling like it's kind of going the other way for me. I start retaining water. So I mean I've just found that maybe it's maybe we're not designed to just like consume that many sweet potatoes, right? Like so I'm trying to get to maybe 200 or 300 grams of carbs on some days just to like I'm I'm really trying to push it and test it, right? I'm trying to you've challenged me on some of this stuff and I'm like okay like I will test it. I will I will go high carb on some of these days and um Yeah. chunks.title: Cultural Perspectives on Diet
chunks.summary: The speakers explore how certain cultures, like the imbu muti tribe, obtain a significant portion of their calories from honey, while still maintaining good health. They reflect on experiences with traditional diets and the challenges of modern dietary practices.
